title: Layer-parity-dependent interfacial coupling in Nb$_3$Cl$_8$/graphene van der Waals heterostructures
authors: Hansheng Xu, Yuchen Gao, Xinyue Huang, Weihanzhang Guo, Zhijie Ma, Ziqi Liu, Pinfan Gu, Kenji Watanabe, Takashi Taniguchi, Youguo Shi, Yu Ye
abstract: Strongly correlated two-dimensional systems provide compelling platforms for investigating exotic quantum phenomena. Niobium chloride (Nb$_3$Cl$_8$), a single-band Mott insulator, exhibits a remarkable out-of-plane polarization in its topmost layer that oscillates with layer parity, manifesting as an odd-even effect. Using atomic force microscopy (AFM) and Kelvin probe force microscopy (KPFM), this layer-parity-dependent polarization can be effectively characterized through surface morphology and potential mapping, enabling the unambiguous identification of different surface phases. We then fabricated dual-gate Hall devices by coupling different surface phases of Nb$_3$Cl$_8$ with monolayer graphene to investigate how the topmost-layer out-of-plane polarization influences interfacial coupling and the resulting transport behavior. Our results reveal significant phase-dependent variations in charge transfer, carrier densities, and hybridization gaps (25.2 meV for Phase 1 and 30.0 meV for Phase 2). Density functional theory calculations corroborate these experimental findings, showing that distinct out-of-plane polarizations in the topmost layer lead to different orbital overlaps and interfacial coupling strengths. These findings highlight the critical importance of surface polarization and orbital orientation in engineering the properties of strongly correlated van der Waals heterostructures.
summary: This work demonstrates that the surface polarization of the Mott insulator $	ext{Nb}_3	ext{Cl}_8$ critically controls its interaction with graphene. By fabricating heterostructures on surfaces exhibiting different polarizations, the authors show distinct electronic coupling strengths and measurable changes in transport gaps, highlighting layer-parity engineering as a tool for controlling correlated material properties.
main_problem: The study investigates how the layer-parity-dependent out-of-plane polarization of a Mott insulator ($	ext{Nb}_3	ext{Cl}_8$) dictates the interfacial coupling strength and electronic properties when coupled with graphene.
main_result: Different surface polarizations lead to distinct coupling strengths, evidenced by measurable differences in hybridization gaps (25.2 meV vs 30.0 meV) and charge transfer magnitudes in transport measurements.
method: The research combines experimental characterization using AFM/KPFM with magnetotransport measurements on fabricated devices, corroborated by DFT calculations.
model_or_system: The system is a van der Waals heterostructure formed by coupling monolayer graphene (MLG) to $	ext{Nb}_3	ext{Cl}_8$, which exhibits layer-parity dependent polarization due to its Mott insulating nature.
key_observables: Hybridization gap ($\Delta$), charge transfer magnitude (inferred from Landau fan slopes), carrier density ($n$), and surface potential/polarization state.
important_parameters: Layer parity (odd vs. even layers), hybridization gap values (25.2 meV, 30.0 meV), and polarization direction (upward vs. downward).
main_assumptions: The coupling mechanism is strongly dependent on the local surface termination/polarization state of the Mott insulator.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate AFM phase maps showing layer-parity dependence, KPFM potential mapping, and transport data (R-T, B-field) comparing devices built on different $	ext{Nb}_3	ext{Cl}_8$ phases.
paper_structure: The paper first establishes the physical phenomenon of layer-parity dependent polarization using AFM/KPFM. It then builds dual-gate Hall devices on these distinct surface phases to measure transport properties, comparing results (e.g., gap size, carrier density) between phases associated with different polarizations.
why_interesting: The explicit link drawn between crystal symmetry breaking (polarization) and tunable electronic coupling in a correlated material platform is highly relevant for designing novel quantum devices based on interfacial engineering.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Layer-parity-dependent out-of-plane polarization in exfoliated Nb3Cl8. (a), Raman spectra of bulk Nb3Cl8 and few-layer exfoliated samples at 2 K, showing characteristic A1 1g (at 173 cm−1) peak of α-phase. Inset: the optical image of the exfoliated Nb3Cl8 flake. (b), Crystal structure of α-Nb3Cl8 showing the kagome lattice with a two-layer periodicity. (c), Schematic illustration of the layer-parity-dependent out-of-plane polarization. The different surface terminations lead to distinct electronic properties when coupled with graphene. (d-e), AFM morphology (d) and phase (e) maps of a representative few-layer Nb3Cl8 at the same region. (f), Height profile along the marked red dashed...

Fig. 2. Device structure and density functional theory calculations. (a), Schematic illustration of the dual-gated Hall bar device showing the layer stacking geometry. (b), Optical microscope image of the double-gate Hall Device 1 and Device 2 (separated by a red dashed line), in which the MLG is located on a few-layer Nb3Cl8 film with one layer thickness difference. (c), DFT-calculated band structure of the Nb3Cl8/MLG heterostructure for Phase 1 (upward polarization), showing the formation of a hybridization gap. (d), DFT-calculated band structure for Phase 2 (downward polarization), displaying a larger hybridization gap compared to Phase 1. Insets show the stacking structures of...

Fig. 3. Magnetotransport behavior of different phases Nb3Cl8/MLG heterostructure devices. (a), Longitudinal resistance Rxx as a function of magnetic field at zero gate voltages, measured at 2 K with the magnetic field applied perpendicular to the sample plane. (b), Hall resistance Rxy versus magnetic field for both devices under the same conditions. (c), Shubnikov–de Haas (SdH) oscillations plotted as Rxx versus 1/B. The oscillation frequencies yield carrier densities of n = 8.25 × 1011 cm−2 (6.12 × 1011 cm−2) for Device 1 and (Device 2). (d), Temperature-dependent carrier density at VTG = 0 V extracted from temperature-dependent Hall measurements. The data are fitted using the Fermi-Dirac...

Fig. 4. Transport behavior of different phases Nb3Cl8/MLG devices under gate and magnetic field regulation. (a), Longitudinal resistance versus VTG at 2 K without applying a magnetic field, showing distinct gate-dependent behaviors reflecting different interfacial coupling strengths. (b-c), Landau fan diagrams showing Rxx as a function of VTG and magnetic field at 2 K for Device 1 and Device 2, respectively. Two distinct regimes separated by red dashed lines are visible. The steeper slope of the Landau fan in Regime I for Device 2 further confirms the larger magnitude of charge transfer, demonstrating the phase-dependent interfacial coupling effect.
